label: verify-prop-26
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Prop 26

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify"…

[tetlock] … This is the threshold gate for the whole dispute. If it's not a property-related fee (the district's alternative argument), §6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality test never applies and the analysis shifts entirely to Prop 26's 'reasonable relationship' fee-exceptions test or to whether voter approval was required and skipped. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vial treats water service charges as property-related fees, but a surcharge laye…

[dialectic] … Cal.5th 1191 held that a *groundwater extraction/pumping* charge imposed on extractors (not directly on property owners as retail customers) is not a property-related fee under Art. XIII D and is instead analyzed under Prop 26/Art. XIII C. That case is inapposite: Sierra Vista's surcharge is a retail volumetric charge on metered water delivered to a specific parcel, squarely within Bighorn/Capistrano, not the wholesale-extraction scenario in …

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (as added/amended by Proposition 26, approved Nov. 2, 2010)","note":"Proposition 26 (2010) is correctly identified as adding Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e), which defines \"tax\" and sets out seven enumerated exceptions for fees/charges, including the requirement that costs allocated to a payor bear a \"fair or reasonable relationship\" to the payor's burden or benefit. The documents' use of \"Prop 26's reasonable relationship fee-exceptions test\" as distinct from Prop 218/Art. XIII D §6(b)'s cost-of-service proportionality test for property-related fees is a standard, accurate characterization consistent with official text and practitioner guides (League of CA Cities, CSDA, Meyers Nave).","source_urls":["https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displayText.xhtml?lawCode=CONS&article=XIII+C","https://meyersnave.com/wp-content/uploads/LOCC-Implementation-Guide-Prop-26-and-218.pdf","https://www.gcsd.org/files/4da2c072f/CSDA+Guide-Proposition+26.pdf","https://www.beninkslavens.com/blog/2021/06/are-property-related-fees-that-exceed-costs-taxes-as-defined-by-proposition-26/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (as added/amended by Proposition 26, approved Nov. 2, 2010)
data.note: Proposition 26 (2010) is correctly identified as adding Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e), which defines "tax" and sets out seven enumerated exceptions for fees/charges, including the requirement that costs allocated to a payor bear a "fair or reasonable relationship" to the payor's burden or benefit. The documents' use of "Prop 26's reasonable relationship fee-exceptions test" as distinct from Prop 218/Art. XIII D §6(b)'s cost-of-service proportionality test for property-related fees is a standard, accurate characterization consistent with official text and practitioner guides (League of CA Cities, CSDA, Meyers Nave).
